progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an important and well-motivated problem—reducing annotation burden for instance segmentation by incorporating non-differentiable high-level priors—and the proposed stateless actor-critic formulation over superpixel graph partitioning is potentially interesting, especially for biomedical imaging where rule-based knowledge is often available. The introduction is generally clear in motivating the gap between differentiable prior-based losses and richer non-differentiable constraints, and the claimed contributions are concrete. However, the framing currently feels somewhat ambitious relative to the evidence provided in the opening: key technical concepts such as “stateless actor-critic,” how the critic approximates object/image-level rewards, and how graph partitioning is embedded in the learning loop need sharper explanation to make the novelty and feasibility convincing. The paper should also be careful with claims such as “end-to-end learning” and “excellent performance without any direct supervision,” since the method appears to depend heavily on hand-designed priors and superpixel quality; the limits of this supervision signal should be acknowledged more explicitly. The related work section is relevant but somewhat narrow, and would benefit from clearer positioning against weakly supervised, constraint-based, energy-based, and classical prior-driven segmentation methods, not only RL and differentiable-loss alternatives. Overall, the paper appears promising and potentially impactful, but its main risks are whether the proposed RL formulation is truly necessary compared with surrogate/black-box optimization alternatives, whether the reward priors generalize beyond carefully structured domains, and whether the experiments demonstrate robustness rather than success on tasks where the priors encode most of the solution.

comments.title: Abstract overstates that non-differentiable rules cannot be used with existing methods
comments.quote: However, these rules are in 8 general not differentiable and thus cannot be used with existing methods.
comments.explanation: The intended point seems to be that arbitrary high-level priors are hard to use as direct gradient-based losses in standard supervised CNN training, and the later related-work section does acknowledge relaxations, surrogate losses, adversarial/surrogate networks, and post-processing approaches. With that context, the motivation is reasonable. What remains problematic is the unqualified statement that such rules 'cannot be used with existing methods.' Non-differentiable priors can be used in post-processing, black-box optimization, reinforcement learning, structured prediction, surrogate/relaxed objectives, or evaluation-driven training schemes. The claim would be more accurate if narrowed to 'cannot be used directly as differentiable losses in standard end-to-end backpropagation.' As written, it overstates the limitation of prior work.

comments.title: Critic terminology conflates reward evaluation with Q/reward approximation
comments.quote: Based on the predicted weights, the segmentation is obtained 45 through (non-differentiable) graph partitioning and the segmented objects are then evaluated by the 46 critic, which learns to approximate the rewards based on the object- and image-level reasoning (see 47 Fig. 1).
comments.explanation: The paper sometimes describes the critic as if it directly evaluates segmented objects using the object- or image-level rules, while the later method and Figure 1 suggest a different pipeline: the environment performs graph partitioning and computes rewards from the segmentation, and the critic learns an approximation to the resulting value/reward signal from states and actions. This distinction matters in actor-critic terminology, where the critic estimates a value or Q-function rather than defining the non-differentiable reward. Relatedly, the statement that the critic learns the rewards 'by Q-learning' is potentially misleading in a stateless, one-step setting where the action value reduces to immediate reward and the critic loss is closer to supervised regression to observed rewards than standard temporal-difference Q-learning. The intended analogy to a learned surrogate is understandable, but the wording could confuse readers about what the critic receives, what computes the reward, and whether a conventional Q-learning update is used.

comments.title: RL avoids differentiating through the pipeline, but does not make the pipeline differentiable end-to-end
comments.quote: Our main motivation to explore RL for the instance segmentation task is to circumvent the restriction 106 to differentiable losses and - regardless of the loss - to make the whole pipeline differentiable end-to107 end even in presence of non-differentiable steps which transform pixelwise CNN predictions into 108 individual instances.
comments.explanation: The intended point is valid: an actor-critic objective can train components upstream of a non-differentiable graph partitioning step and can use rewards that are themselves non-differentiable. However, the quoted sentence goes further and says the method makes the whole pipeline 'differentiable end-to-end.' In standard policy-gradient/actor-critic training, gradients are not propagated through the environment transition, graph partitioning, or reward computation; the actor is updated through likelihood-ratio or critic-based gradients. Thus the overall image-to-instance-to-reward process is trainable end-to-end in a loose sense, but it is not differentiable end-to-end. A safer formulation would be that the method enables end-to-end optimization despite non-differentiable components.

comments.title: Multicut use is underspecified for actions constrained to [0,1]
comments.quote: Given edge weights _W_ , an instance 112 segmentation can be obtained by partitioning the graph, here using an approximate multicut solver 113 [37].
comments.explanation: The high-level formulation is clear: the actor predicts edge-associated quantities and graph partitioning turns them into instances. What remains unclear is the precise interface between the actor outputs and the multicut solver. Later in the same passage the actions are described as 'real-valued edge weights in [0, 1].' In the standard minimum-cost multicut formulation, edge costs usually have signs: positive costs discourage cutting an edge and negative costs encourage cutting it, depending on the convention. If all supplied costs are simply in [0,1], a standard multicut objective may have a trivial optimum under common conventions unless the weights are transformed, interpreted as affinities/probabilities, or used in a nonstandard objective. The passage does not specify whether the [0,1] actions are converted to signed costs, log-odds, boundary probabilities, merge affinities, or thresholds before partitioning. This is a key reproducibility detail for the environment step.

comments.title: State-transition description is unclear for a nominally stateless, one-step setup
comments.quote: Any state can be reached by a single step from the initial state and 133 there exists no time dependency in the state transition.
comments.explanation: The intended contrast with sequential RL methods is understandable: the agent predicts all graph edge values at once, receives a reward for the resulting segmentation, and the episode terminates. In that sense this is closer to a contextual bandit or one-step MDP than to a temporal decision process. However, the quoted statement is hard to reconcile with the definitions given immediately before it. The state appears to be the image/superpixel graph/features, while the action is the vector of edge weights used for partitioning. Actions do not seem to move the system from an initial state to arbitrary image states; rather, each training image supplies a context/state and the episode ends after one action. If 'state' here means 'segmentation outcome' or 'environment configuration,' that differs from the earlier definition of state. The later sentence about episodes of length 1 helps, but this particular claim could mislead readers about the MDP being formulated.

comments.title: Claim that stateless RL was introduced in a recent GAN-related work is historically too strong
comments.quote: To the best of our knowledge, stateless RL was introduced in [7] to study the connection between 137 generative adversarial networks and actor critics and our method is one of the first practical applica138 tions of this concept.
comments.explanation: The authors are likely referring to a particular 'stateless actor-critic' formalization used to relate GANs and actor-critic methods, and in that narrow sense the citation may be relevant. Still, the wording 'stateless RL was introduced in [7]' is too broad. Single-state MDPs, multi-armed bandits, and contextual bandits are longstanding RL settings that predate GAN-related actor-critic interpretations by decades. The subsequent claim that this is one of the first practical applications of 'this concept' would be more defensible if explicitly limited to the specific stateless actor-critic/GAN-theoretic formulation of [7], rather than stateless RL generally.

comments.title: Squashed-Gaussian policy probability is underspecified for the actor loss
comments.quote: The actor corresponds to a single GNN, which predicts the mean and variance of a normal distribution 146 for each edge. The actions _a_ are determined by sampling from this distribution and applying a 147 sigmoid to the result to obtain continuous edge weights in the value range [0 _,_ 1].
comments.explanation: The intended construction is clear and standard in spirit: sample unconstrained Gaussian edge logits and squash them to obtain valid edge weights in [0,1]. However, the subsequent actor update depends on the policy probability over sub-graph actions. For a squashed Gaussian, the density of the actual bounded action values is not just the Gaussian density of the pre-sigmoid samples; it requires a change-of-variables/Jacobian correction, with factors involving a(1-a) per edge. If the authors compute the joint probability over the pre-sigmoid variables, that should be stated; if they compute it over the bounded edge weights, the Jacobian term is needed for the KL/entropy interpretation they describe. The text also says the GNN predicts a variance, but does not say how positivity is enforced, e.g. by predicting log-variance or applying softplus/clamping. These details make the policy density used in the actor loss ambiguous enough that readers could implement a different objective.

comments.title: Sub-graph critic MLP appears to require an unspecified edge ordering and may break permutation invariance
comments.quote: Based on these edge features _Qsg_ is predicted for each sub-graph via an 162 MLP. Here, we use a set of subgraph sizes (typically, 6, 12, 32, 128) to generate a supervison signal ... 163 for different neighborhood scales. A given MLP is only valid for a fixed graph size, so we employ a 164 different MLP for each size.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly recognize that a plain MLP has a fixed input dimensionality, motivating separate heads for sub-graphs with different numbers of edges. What remains unclear is how the set of edge feature vectors in a sub-graph is converted into the ordered vector consumed by the MLP. Graph edges are unordered, and different orderings of the same sub-graph should not generally change the predicted action value. If the edge features are simply concatenated, the critic is not permutation invariant unless a canonical edge ordering is defined and consistently used. If instead the authors use pooling, sorting, positional encodings, or another invariant/equivariant construction, that key step is not described here. This matters both for reproducibility and for the validity of the learned sub-graph Q-values.

comments.title: Local reward decomposition and object-to-subgraph mapping are not sufficiently justified
comments.quote: Of course then requirement arises that the union of 173 local rewards must resemble to the global reward. E.g. the optimal policy is the same for local as for 174 the global reward.
comments.explanation: The paper appropriately notes that replacing a global reward by local sub-graph rewards is not innocuous: in general, reward decompositions can change the optimal policy unless they preserve the relevant objective. However, the proposed object-to-edge-to-subgraph mapping is then introduced as a heuristic without showing that this condition holds even approximately. The mapping assigns each superpixel its object reward, assigns an edge the maximum of its two incident superpixel rewards, and averages edge rewards over sub-graphs. This can alter credit assignment relative to the image-level or object-level prior: low-quality objects may be masked by adjacent high-scoring objects, many correct internal merge decisions inside a bad object may be penalized, and overlapping sub-graphs may weight some decisions more than others. The authors acknowledge noise and give plausible motivation, but important behavior remains underspecified, including whether boundary/cut and internal/merge edges are treated differently, whether rewards are normalized for sub-graph overlap, and how background objects interact with the rule.

comments.title: The multicut motivation overstates the guarantee when an approximate solver is used
comments.quote: To transform the edge weight predictions of the agent into an instance segmentation we use the 211 Multicut [42] algorithm. Here, other options are also possible such as hierarchical clustering used in 212 [9], but we choose the Multicut for its global optimality property.
comments.explanation: The motivation is understandable: multicut provides a global graph-partitioning objective, unlike some greedy agglomerative procedures. However, earlier context says the segmentation is obtained using an approximate multicut solver, and the multicut/correlation clustering problem is NP-hard in general. Therefore the implemented procedure does not necessarily have a global optimality guarantee. If the authors mean that the multicut formulation defines a global energy over all edges, rather than a sequence of local merge decisions, that should be phrased as a global objective/formulation rather than a global optimality property.

comments.title: CHT-derived object score is not specified enough to reproduce
comments.quote: As we aim to segment disks, we compute the circularity of the segmented objects for the rewards 222 using the Circle Hough Transform [43].
comments.explanation: The choice of a Circle Hough Transform is reasonable for disk-like synthetic objects, and one can infer that the authors want a normalized measure of how circle-like each predicted object is. What remains unclear is how the scalar CHT value for an already segmented object is computed. A CHT normally depends on choices such as the radius range, accumulator normalization, edge/mask preprocessing, whether the maximum accumulator value or some aggregate is used, and how object size affects the score. The text later states that c ∈ [0,1] is 'the CHT value corresponding to the object,' but without these details a reader could implement substantially different reward functions with different behavior. This is especially important because the reward, rather than labels, is the supervision signal.

comments.title: Count prior parameter k is underspecified as a source of prior information
comments.quote: This object-level reward is combined with the global rough 223 estimate of the number of objects in the image to create the reward surface depicted in Fig. 3.
comments.explanation: The idea of using a rough object-count prior is consistent with the paper's broader goal of exploiting high-level knowledge rather than pixel labels. Still, the passage does not say how this estimate is obtained in the synthetic experiment: whether k is the exact generated number of disks per image, a fixed dataset-level expected count, a noisy prior, or a manually supplied range. These alternatives differ substantially in supervision strength. If the exact per-image count from the simulator is used, the setup is still rule-based but uses privileged information that would be unavailable in many real applications; if only a rough fixed estimate is used, the method is weaker but more realistic. Clarifying this would be important for interpreting the synthetic results and for reproducing the reward surface.

comments.title: Foreground/background count convention and largest-background heuristic are underspecified
comments.quote: Let _c ∈_ [0 _,_ 1] be the CHT value corresponding 230 to the object and let _k_ be the total number of objects that we expect and _n_ be the number of predicted 231 objects.
comments.explanation: The reward design appears to reward foreground objects for being circular while using an image-level count prior to discourage over- or under-segmentation. However, the later text says the largest predicted object is treated as background, which makes it unclear whether n counts all connected components or only predicted foreground disks. If n includes the background, then a correct segmentation with k disks plus one background component would have n = k+1 unless k also includes background. If the largest component is removed before counting, that should be stated explicitly. The largest-object-as-background heuristic also needs clearer boundary conditions, since early poor segmentations may merge background with disks or split the background. Because background handling interacts with both foreground object rewards and the count term, this convention could systematically bias training if implemented inconsistently.

comments.title: Claim that this reward setup is not fit for Method 1 is stronger than justified here
comments.quote: Note that this rewards have a large globally calculated part which makes this setup not fit for Method 238 1. It needs some feature representation that already gives a good idea for the clustering.
comments.explanation: The authors' point is understandable: if much of the reward is global, then a randomly initialized feature extractor in Method 1 may receive too weak or noisy a learning signal, whereas Method 2 starts from pretrained features that already make graph clustering meaningful. However, the wording 'makes this setup not fit for Method 1' reads like a general methodological conclusion, while the passage only gives an intuitive explanation. The method still contains an object-level CHT component that is later mapped to local subgraph rewards, so it is not obvious from the description alone that Method 1 could not work, only that it may be harder or empirically inferior. This would be better framed as an empirical observation or design choice unless supported by an ablation.

comments.title: Model selection appears to use the test set
comments.quote: For comparison we keep the model which achieved the highest reward on the test set. This makes training as well as the validation independent from ground truth annotations.
comments.explanation: The intended point may be that no ground-truth labels are used for checkpoint selection, only the rule-based reward. That is important for the paper's weak/no-supervision claim. However, the phrase 'highest reward on the test set' is problematic. Standard experimental practice is to choose hyperparameters/checkpoints on a training or validation set and reserve the test set for final unbiased evaluation. The surrounding text then discusses 'validation scores' and reward evolution 'on the validation set,' so it is possible the authors meant validation set rather than test set. If so, this should be corrected. As written it suggests test-set model selection; even without ground truth, selecting the checkpoint by test-set reward can bias reported performance toward the held-out images.

comments.title: Augmentation-noise variant is underspecified despite being the best reported result
comments.quote: (ours + 305 augmentation noise) is the same method but add some noise to the input data during training
comments.explanation: This variant gives the best SBD score in Table 1, so the exact modification matters for reproducibility and for interpreting the comparison. 'Add some noise to the input data' leaves several important choices unspecified: the distribution, magnitude, whether the noise is applied to raw pixels, learned feature maps, hand-crafted node features, edge-prediction inputs, or all of these, and whether it is used only during training or also for reward/model selection. If the appendix gives these details, a pointer would resolve the issue; otherwise this is a meaningful methodological gap because the reported improvement over the base variant may depend strongly on this augmentation choice.

comments.title: Description of VI components uses misleading terminology
comments.quote: For the validation scores we use the variation of information (VI) for both input combinations (merge 301 and split) and the Symmetric Best Dice score.
comments.explanation: The intended evaluation is mostly clear from Table 1, which reports 'VI merge' and 'VI split,' the standard decomposition of variation of information into over- and under-segmentation-related terms. However, calling merge and split 'input combinations' is technically misleading: they are not input combinations but components of the VI metric. This is likely just a wording error, but rephrasing to 'the merge and split components of VI' would remove the ambiguity.

comments.title: Claim of no direct supervision is weakened by use of pretrained edge predictions
comments.quote: we showed in particular that our setup can segment microscopy images with no direct supervision other than high-level reasoning.
comments.explanation: The authors are careful throughout the paper to distinguish reward supervision from direct pixelwise ground truth, and in that sense the RL training itself appears to be driven by high-level rules. However, in the microscopy experiment the method also uses superpixels created from boundary/edge predictions, and Table 1 includes variants such as 'ours without edges,' described as removing 'the additional edge prediction as an input.' The baselines further identify these as boundary predictions from [47]. If those edge predictions come from a supervised pretrained boundary detector, then the complete segmentation pipeline is not using only high-level reasoning: it also depends on learned boundary information that encodes external direct supervision, even if not from the target validation set. The claim would be more accurate if qualified as 'no target-domain ground-truth supervision for training the RL agent,' or if the authors explicitly state that the edge predictor/superpixel generator is unsupervised or otherwise not trained with instance/boundary annotations.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an important and well-motivated problem—reducing annotation burden for instance segmentation by incorporating non-differentiable high-level priors—and the proposed stateless actor-critic formulation over superpixel graph partitioning is potentially interesting, especially for biomedical imaging where rule-based knowledge is often available. The introduction is generally clear in motivating the gap between differentiable prior-based losses and richer non-differentiable constraints, and the claimed contributions are concrete. However, the framing currently feels somewhat ambitious relative to the evidence provided in the opening: key technical concepts such as “stateless actor-critic,” how the critic approximates object/image-level rewards, and how graph partitioning is embedded in the learning loop need sharper explanation to make the novelty and feasibility convincing. The paper should also be careful with claims such as “end-to-end learning” and “excellent performance without any direct supervision,” since the method appears to depend heavily on hand-designed priors and superpixel quality; the limits of this supervision signal should be acknowledged more explicitly. The related work section is relevant but somewhat narrow, and would benefit from clearer positioning against weakly supervised, constraint-based, energy-based, and classical prior-driven segmentation methods, not only RL and differentiable-loss alternatives. Overall, the paper appears promising and potentially impactful, but its main risks are whether the proposed RL formulation is truly necessary compared with surrogate/black-box optimization alternatives, whether the reward priors generalize beyond carefully structured domains, and whether the experiments demonstrate robustness rather than success on tasks where the priors encode most of the solution.

comments.title: Critic is described as evaluating segmented objects rather than approximating rewards
comments.quote: Based on the predicted weights, the segmentation is obtained 45 through (non-differentiable) graph partitioning and the segmented objects are then evaluated by the 46 critic, which learns to approximate the rewards based on the object- and image-level reasoning (see 47 Fig. 1).
comments.explanation: I initially read this as saying that the critic itself takes the segmented objects and applies the object- or image-level rules to score them. Looking at the later method description, the intended pipeline appears different: the environment performs graph partitioning and computes rewards from the resulting segmentation, while the critic learns an approximation to the action value/reward from the state and actions. Figure 1 also says rewards are obtained by evaluating the segmentation and then given back to the agent. Thus the broad idea is clear from context, but this sentence conflates the hand-designed reward/evaluation function with the learned critic. In actor-critic terminology, the critic estimates a value or Q-function; it is not normally the component that defines or directly computes the non-differentiable reward. This could mislead readers about what inputs the critic receives and where the high-level priors are implemented.

comments.title: Abstract overstates that non-differentiable rules cannot be used with existing methods
comments.quote: However, these rules are in 8 general not differentiable and thus cannot be used with existing methods.
comments.explanation: The authors’ intended point seems to be that arbitrary high-level priors are hard to use as direct gradient-based losses in standard supervised CNN training, and the later related-work section does acknowledge relaxations, surrogate losses, adversarial/surrogate networks, and post-processing approaches. With that context, the motivation is reasonable. What remains problematic is the unqualified statement that such rules 'cannot be used with existing methods.' Non-differentiable priors can be used in post-processing, black-box optimization, reinforcement learning, structured prediction, surrogate/relaxed objectives, or evaluation-driven training schemes. The claim would be more accurate if narrowed to 'cannot be used directly as differentiable losses in standard end-to-end backpropagation.' As written, it overstates the limitation of prior work.

comments.title: Novelty claim about stateless actor-critic applications appears underqualified
comments.quote: We base 41 our framework on a stateless actor-critic setup [7], providing one of the first practical applications 42 of this important theoretical construct.
comments.explanation: I understand that the authors may be referring to a particular stateless actor-critic formulation from the cited work, and the later methods clarify that their episodes have length one. Still, the claim that this is 'one of the first practical applications' is difficult to accept without qualification. Stateless or one-step RL formulations are closely related to bandit and contextual-bandit settings, which have many practical applications, and actor-critic/value-estimation ideas have also been used in simplified or one-step decision settings. If the intended novelty is the application of the specific stateless actor-critic construction of [7] to instance segmentation, that should be stated more narrowly. Otherwise the sentence risks overstating the historical novelty of the RL setup.

comments.title: RL avoids differentiating through the pipeline, but does not make the pipeline differentiable end-to-end
comments.quote: Our main motivation to explore RL for the instance segmentation task is to circumvent the restriction 106 to differentiable losses and - regardless of the loss - to make the whole pipeline differentiable end-to107 end even in presence of non-differentiable steps which transform pixelwise CNN predictions into 108 individual instances.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended point: by using an actor-critic objective, the authors can train components upstream of a non-differentiable graph partitioning step and can use rewards that are themselves non-differentiable. That is a valid motivation, and later text correctly says the non-differentiable “pixels to instances” step is encapsulated in the environment. However, the quoted sentence goes further and says the approach makes the whole pipeline “differentiable end-to-end.” In standard policy-gradient/actor-critic training, gradients are not propagated through the environment transition, graph partitioning, or reward computation; instead, the actor is updated through likelihood-ratio or critic-based gradients. Thus the overall image-to-instance-to-reward computation is trainable end-to-end in a loose sense, but it is not differentiable end-to-end. This distinction matters because the paper’s central motivation is precisely the presence of non-differentiable steps. A safer formulation would be that the method enables end-to-end training/optimization despite non-differentiable components, not that it makes the pipeline differentiable.

comments.title: Multicut use is underspecified for actions constrained to [0,1]
comments.quote: Given edge weights _W_ , an instance 112 segmentation can be obtained by partitioning the graph, here using an approximate multicut solver 113 [37].
comments.explanation: The high-level formulation is clear: the actor predicts edge-associated quantities and graph partitioning turns them into instances. What remains unclear is the precise interface between the actor outputs and the multicut solver. Later in the same passage the actions are described as “real-valued edge weights in [0, 1].” In the standard minimum-cost multicut formulation, edge costs usually have signs: positive costs discourage cutting an edge and negative costs encourage cutting it, depending on the convention. If all supplied costs are simply in [0,1], a standard multicut objective may have a trivial optimum under common conventions unless the weights are transformed, interpreted as affinities/probabilities, or used in a nonstandard objective. The passage does not specify whether the [0,1] actions are converted to signed costs, log-odds, boundary probabilities, merge affinities, or thresholds before partitioning. This is not merely notation; it is a key reproducibility detail for the environment step.

comments.title: State-transition description is unclear for a nominally stateless, one-step setup
comments.quote: Any state can be reached by a single step from the initial state and 133 there exists no time dependency in the state transition.
comments.explanation: I can see the intended contrast with sequential RL methods: the agent predicts all graph edge values at once, receives a reward for the resulting segmentation, and the episode terminates. In that sense this is closer to a contextual bandit or one-step MDP than to a temporal decision process. However, the quoted statement is hard to reconcile with the definitions given immediately before it. The state appears to be the image/superpixel graph/features, while the action is the vector of edge weights used for partitioning. Actions do not seem to move the system from an initial state to arbitrary image states; rather, each training image supplies a context/state and the episode ends after one action. If “state” here means “segmentation outcome” or “environment configuration,” that differs from the earlier definition of state. The later sentence about episodes of length 1 helps, but this particular claim could mislead readers about the MDP being formulated.

comments.title: Claim that stateless RL was introduced in a recent GAN-related work is historically too strong
comments.quote: To the best of our knowledge, stateless RL was introduced in [7] to study the connection between 137 generative adversarial networks and actor critics and our method is one of the first practical applica138 tions of this concept.
comments.explanation: The authors are likely referring to a particular “stateless actor-critic” formalization used to relate GANs and actor-critic methods, and in that narrow sense the citation may be relevant. Still, the wording “stateless RL was introduced in [7]” is too broad. Single-state MDPs, multi-armed bandits, and contextual bandits are longstanding RL settings that predate GAN-related actor-critic interpretations by decades. Even with the qualifier “to the best of our knowledge,” a careful reader may object that the broader concept of stateless or one-step RL was not introduced there. The subsequent claim that this is one of the first practical applications of “this concept” would be more defensible if explicitly limited to the specific stateless actor-critic/GAN-theoretic formulation of [7], rather than stateless RL generally.

comments.title: Calling the critic’s regression to one-step rewards “Q-learning” may be misleading
comments.quote: In our setup, the critic can informally be viewed as a surrogate network 84 as it learns to approximate the priors through the rewards by Q-learning.
comments.explanation: The analogy to surrogate losses is understandable: the critic learns a smooth/parametric approximation to a reward signal derived from non-differentiable priors. However, later the method is described as stateless with episodes of length 1, where the action value reduces to the immediate reward rather than a bootstrapped estimate of a discounted return. In that setting, the critic loss is essentially supervised regression to observed rewards, not necessarily Q-learning in the usual temporal-difference sense involving Bellman targets and bootstrapping over next states. If the authors use “Q-learning” informally to mean learning a Q-function, the intent is recoverable, but the wording could mislead readers into expecting a standard Q-learning update.

comments.title: Squashed-Gaussian policy probability is underspecified for the actor loss
comments.quote: The actor corresponds to a single GNN, which predicts the mean and variance of a normal distribution 146 for each edge. The actions _a_ are determined by sampling from this distribution and applying a 147 sigmoid to the result to obtain continuous edge weights in the value range [0 _,_ 1].
comments.explanation: The intended construction is clear and standard in spirit: sample unconstrained Gaussian edge logits and squash them to obtain valid edge weights in [0,1]. However, the subsequent actor update depends on the policy probability over sub-graph actions. For a squashed Gaussian, the density of the actual action values is not just the Gaussian density of the pre-sigmoid samples; it requires the change-of-variables/Jacobian correction, i.e. factors involving \(a(1-a)\) per edge. This is analogous to the tanh-squashed Gaussian correction used in soft actor-critic. If the authors compute the joint probability over the pre-sigmoid variables, that should be stated; if they compute it over the bounded edge weights, the Jacobian term is needed for the KL/entropy interpretation they describe. The text also says the GNN predicts a variance, but does not say how positivity is enforced, e.g. by predicting log-variance or applying softplus/clamping. These details may be present in the missing displayed equation or appendix, but as written the policy density used in the actor loss is ambiguous enough that a reader could implement a different objective from the one claimed.

comments.title: Sub-graph critic MLP appears to require an unspecified edge ordering and may break permutation invariance
comments.quote:
Based on these edge features _Qsg_ is predicted for each sub-graph via an 162 MLP. Here, we use a set of subgraph sizes (typically, 6, 12, 32, 128) to generate a supervison signal

4

Figure 2: The graph is subdivided into subgraphs, each sub-graph is highlighted by a different color. All sub-graphs have the same number of edges (here 3). Overall, we use a variety of sizes covering different notions of locality.

Figure 3: An example reward landscape Circle Hough Transform (CHT) rewards. High rewards are given if the overall number of predicted objects is not too high and if the respective object has a large CHT value. We found an exponential gradient of the reward landscape to work best.

163 for different neighborhood scales. A given MLP is only valid for a fixed graph size, so we employ a 164 different MLP for each size.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly recognize that a plain MLP has a fixed input dimensionality, motivating separate heads for sub-graphs with different numbers of edges. What remains unclear is how the set of edge feature vectors in a sub-graph is converted into the ordered vector consumed by the MLP. Graph edges are unordered, and different orderings of the same sub-graph should not generally change the predicted action value. If the edge features are simply concatenated, the critic is not permutation invariant unless a canonical edge ordering is defined and consistently used. If instead the authors use pooling, sorting, positional encodings, or another invariant/equivariant construction, that key step is not described here. This matters both for reproducibility and for the validity of the learned \(Q_{sg}\), because arbitrary extraction-order artifacts could affect the critic targets and actor gradients.

comments.title: Reward decomposition is acknowledged to require preserving the global optimum, but the proposed mapping is not shown to do so
comments.quote: Of course then requirement arises that the union of 173 local rewards must resemble to the global reward. E.g. the optimal policy is the same for local as for 174 the global reward.
comments.explanation: The authors are appropriately cautious in noting that replacing a global reward by local sub-graph rewards is not innocuous: in general, reward decompositions can change the optimal policy unless they preserve the relevant objective. However, the paper then proceeds with a particular object-to-edge-to-subgraph mapping without demonstrating that this condition holds, even approximately. The later explanation makes the heuristic motivation plausible, especially the desire to avoid assigning low object-level rewards to boundary edges that may actually represent correct splits. Still, taking maxima over incident object rewards and then averaging over sub-graphs can clearly alter credit assignment relative to an image-level or object-level prior. For example, errors responsible for a low-quality object may be masked on edges adjacent to high-scoring objects, and overlapping sub-graphs may weight some decisions more than others. Empirical success may justify the heuristic, but the text states a fairly strong requirement and does not provide an argument that the proposed construction satisfies it.

comments.title: Object-to-subgraph reward mapping gives a plausible heuristic but leaves important credit-assignment behavior underspecified
comments.quote: We use the following 186 general procedure to map object-level rewards to sub-graphs: first assign to each superpixel the 187 reward of its corresponding object, then determine the reward per edge as the maximum value of its 188 two incident superpixels’ rewards and average the edge rewards to obtain the reward per sub-graph.
comments.explanation: I can see the intended rationale: high-quality objects should reinforce the actions that produced them, while low-quality object scores are hard to localize to a specific erroneous edge. The subsequent prose partially addresses this. What remains problematic is that the mapping is a central part of the learning signal, yet several consequential details are left implicit. For edges between two predicted instances, the edge reward is based only on the maximum of the two object scores, independent of whether the predicted edge action actually cut or merged the objects, and independent of whether one of the adjacent objects is a large background region. For edges inside a bad object, all incident superpixels inherit the same low object reward, which may penalize many correct internal merge decisions. The authors acknowledge noise, but do not specify whether rewards are normalized for sub-graph overlap, whether boundary/cut and internal/merge edges are treated differently, or how background objects interact with this rule in the general case. This makes the method harder to reproduce and weakens the claim that the local rewards provide action-specific supervision.

comments.title: The multicut motivation overstates the guarantee when an approximate solver is used
comments.quote: To transform the edge weight predictions of the agent into an instance segmentation we use the 211 Multicut [42] algorithm. Here, other options are also possible such as hierarchical clustering used in 212 [9], but we choose the Multicut for its global optimality property.
comments.explanation: The motivation is understandable: multicut provides a global graph-partitioning objective, unlike some greedy agglomerative procedures. However, earlier context says the segmentation is obtained using an approximate multicut solver, and the multicut/correlation clustering problem is NP-hard in general. Therefore the implemented procedure does not necessarily have a global optimality guarantee. If the authors mean that the multicut formulation defines a global energy over all edges, rather than a sequence of local merge decisions, that should be phrased as a global objective/formulation rather than a global optimality property. As written, a reader could reasonably infer a stronger optimization guarantee than the method actually provides.

comments.title: Ambiguity over whether predicted object count includes the background
comments.quote: Let _c ∈_ [0 _,_ 1] be the CHT value corresponding 230 to the object and let _k_ be the total number of objects that we expect and _n_ be the number of predicted 231 objects.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended reward design: foreground objects should be rewarded for being circular, while an image-level count prior discourages severe over- or under-segmentation. However, the later sentence says that the largest predicted object is treated as background, which suggests that the relevant count should probably be the number of predicted foreground disks, not the total number of connected components/instances in the partition. If _n_ literally includes the background component, then a correct segmentation with _k_ disks plus one background object would have _n=k+1_ and would be penalized unless _k_ is also defined to include background. Conversely, if the largest component is removed before counting, that should be stated explicitly. This matters because the count term is described as a large global component of the reward, so an off-by-one or inconsistent foreground/background convention could systematically bias training.

comments.title: CHT-derived object score is not specified enough to reproduce
comments.quote: As we aim to segment disks, we compute the circularity of the segmented objects for the rewards 222 using the Circle Hough Transform [43].
comments.explanation: The choice of a Circle Hough Transform is reasonable for disk-like synthetic objects, and I can infer that the authors want a normalized measure of how circle-like each predicted object is. What remains unclear is how the scalar CHT value for an already segmented object is computed. A CHT normally depends on choices such as the radius range, accumulator normalization, edge/mask preprocessing, whether the maximum accumulator value or some aggregate is used, and how object size affects the score. The text later states that _c ∈ [0,1]_ is 'the CHT value corresponding to the object', but without these details a reader could implement substantially different reward functions with different behavior. This is especially important because the reward, rather than labels, is the supervision signal.

comments.title: Count prior parameter _k_ is underspecified as a source of prior information
comments.quote: This object-level reward is combined with the global rough 223 estimate of the number of objects in the image to create the reward surface depicted in Fig. 3.
comments.explanation: The idea of using a rough object-count prior is consistent with the paper's broader goal of exploiting high-level knowledge rather than pixel labels. Still, the passage does not say how this estimate is obtained in the synthetic experiment: whether _k_ is the exact generated number of disks per image, a fixed dataset-level expected count, a noisy prior, or a manually supplied range. These alternatives differ substantially in supervision strength. If the exact per-image count from the simulator is used, the setup is still rule-based but uses privileged information that would be unavailable in many real applications; if only a rough fixed estimate is used, the method is weaker but more realistic. Clarifying this would be important for interpreting the synthetic results and for reproducing the reward surface.

comments.title: Heuristic identification of the background object needs boundary conditions
comments.quote: For the largest predicted object we strongly suspect the background object. For this object background 236 rewards _rbg_ are calculated by
comments.explanation: It is plausible in the circles-on-background dataset that the largest predicted segment is usually background, so the heuristic is understandable. However, the reward itself can influence learning in early stages when segmentations may be very poor: for example, a large merged component containing background plus several disks, or an incorrectly split background, may violate the assumption. The passage does not specify what happens in those cases or whether the largest-object heuristic is applied before or after filtering foreground candidates for the count reward. Since this background treatment interacts directly with foreground rewards and the count prior, a short clarification of the intended failure-mode behavior would make the method less ambiguous.

comments.title: Claim that this reward setup is not fit for Method 1 is stronger than justified here
comments.quote: Note that this rewards have a large globally calculated part which makes this setup not fit for Method 238 1. It needs some feature representation that already gives a good idea for the clustering.
comments.explanation: I see the authors' point: if much of the reward is global, then a randomly initialized feature extractor in Method 1 may receive too weak or noisy a learning signal, whereas Method 2 starts from pretrained features that already make the graph clustering meaningful. That said, the wording 'makes this setup not fit for Method 1' reads like a general methodological conclusion, while the passage only gives an intuitive explanation. The method still contains an object-level CHT component that is later mapped to local subgraph rewards, so it is not obvious from the description alone that Method 1 could not work, only that it may be harder or empirically inferior. This would be better framed as an empirical observation or design choice unless supported by an ablation.

comments.title: Model selection appears to use the test set
comments.quote: For comparison we keep the model which achieved the highest reward on the test set. This makes training as well as the validation independent from ground truth annotations.
comments.explanation: I first read this as an attempt to emphasize that no ground-truth labels are used for checkpoint selection, only the rule-based reward. That is a valid and important point for this paper’s weak/no-supervision claim. However, the phrase “highest reward on the test set” remains problematic. Standard experimental practice is to choose hyperparameters/checkpoints on a training or validation set and reserve the test set for final unbiased evaluation. The surrounding text then discusses “validation scores” and reward evolution “on the validation set,” so it is possible the authors meant validation set rather than test set. If so, this should be corrected because as written it suggests test-set model selection. Even without using ground truth, selecting the checkpoint by test-set reward can bias reported test/validation performance toward the specific held-out images and weakens the evaluation protocol.

comments.title: Claim of no direct supervision is weakened by use of pretrained edge predictions
comments.quote: we showed in particular that our setup can segment microscopy images with no direct supervision other than high-level reasoning.
comments.explanation: The authors are careful throughout the paper to distinguish the reward supervision from direct pixelwise ground truth, and in that sense the RL training itself appears to be driven by high-level rules. However, in this microscopy experiment the method also uses superpixels created from boundary/edge predictions, and Table 1 includes variants such as “ours without edges,” described as removing “the additional edge prediction as an input.” The baselines further identify these as boundary predictions from [47]. If those edge predictions come from a supervised pretrained boundary detector, then the complete segmentation pipeline is not using only high-level reasoning: it also depends on learned boundary information that encodes external direct supervision, even if not from the target validation set. The claim would be more accurate if qualified as “no target-domain ground-truth supervision for training the RL agent,” or if the authors explicitly state that the edge predictor/superpixel generator is unsupervised or otherwise not trained with instance/boundary annotations.

comments.title: Augmentation-noise variant is underspecified despite being the best reported result
comments.quote: (ours + 305 augmentation noise) is the same method but add some noise to the input data during training
comments.explanation: I understand that this paragraph is summarizing variants and that some hyperparameters may be deferred to the appendix. Still, this variant gives the best SBD score in Table 1, so the exact modification matters for reproducibility and for interpreting the comparison. “Add some noise to the input data” leaves several important choices unspecified: the distribution, magnitude, whether the noise is applied to raw pixels, learned feature maps, hand-crafted node features, edge-prediction inputs, or all of these, and whether it is used only during training or also for reward/model selection. If the appendix gives these details, a pointer would resolve the issue; otherwise this is a meaningful methodological gap because the reported improvement over “ours” may depend strongly on this augmentation choice.

comments.title: Description of VI components uses misleading terminology
comments.quote: For the validation scores we use the variation of information (VI) for both input combinations (merge 301 and split) and the Symmetric Best Dice score.
comments.explanation: The intended evaluation is mostly clear from Table 1, which reports “VI merge” and “VI split,” the standard decomposition of variation of information into over- and under-segmentation-related terms. However, calling merge and split “input combinations” is technically misleading: they are not input combinations but components of the VI metric. This is likely just a wording error, but a careful reader could momentarily wonder whether different input configurations are being evaluated. Rephrasing to “the merge and split components of VI” would remove the ambiguity.
